“Perform Like Steve Jobs“
WORKSHOP WORKBOOK

-- 1 of 13 --

Introduction: Recognizing Steve Jobs as a
Storyteller
Steve Jobs is universally acknowledged as one of the greatest storytellers in business
history. Despite this recognition, many fail to implement his strategies when presenting
products or keynotes. Understanding Jobs as a storyteller is crucial, going beyond the
overused term in business.
Jobs' Macworld 2007 iPhone launch is a pivotal moment in business history, showcasing his
storytelling prowess. By decoding specific segments, we can unveil the depth and
complexity of his storytelling techniques.
Storytelling in business is more than a buzzword; it involves entertaining audiences through
stakes, suspense, surprise, humor, and providing new and impactful information. Jobs'
strategies align with those used by storytellers in various mediums, emphasizing the
importance of entertainment in communication.
“Perform Like Steve Jobs” Workshop © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks Page: 1

-- 2 of 13 --

Lesson 1: Demonstrate confidence from the
start
Jobs begins with extraordinary confidence, showcasing a slow, methodical approach.
Confidence is a key element for effective presentations, inspiring trust, and engagement
from the audience.
The most important part of confidence:
You need not possess confidence to demonstrate confidence. When you know what
confidence looks like, demonstrating it is as simple as taking the actions necessary to
convince your audience that you are confident, regardless of how you actually feel inside.
TIP: IT'S YOU PLUS YOUR IDEA
People don't believe in ideas. They believe in people who have ideas. fI your intention is to
take the stage and share an idea without also sharing yourself, connecting to the audience,
revealing some aspect of your life, and perhaps most important, entertaining the masses,
you shouldn't bother taking the stage at all.
First, you win their hearts and minds by allowing your audience to know you, believe in you,
and maybe even love you.
Only then do you share your idea.
If your talk is so bereft of YOU that it could be delivered by anyone, iti might as well be
delivered by no one. Your talk should be so personally relevant and deeply authentic to who
you are that no one else could possibly deliver it in your absence.
“Perform Like Steve Jobs” Workshop © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks Page: 2

-- 3 of 13 --

Lesson 2: Set Stakes Early to Engage
Audiences, and Build Stakes to Ensure
Continued Engagement
Setting stakes at the beginning of a presentation is crucial. Jobs effectively uses the
"two-year wait" as a stake, signaling to the audience that something significant is about to be
revealed.
He later builds these stakes by promising a revolutionary product. Jobs understands that all
great stories ratchet up the stakes as the story moves along. He does this in his story, too.
TIP: START WITH A MOMENT OF MEANING
In medias res: (Latin) the practice of beginning an epic or other narrative by plunging into a
crucial situation that is part of a related chain of events; the situation is an extension of
previous events and will be developed in later action.
This is a term (and a practice) that every storyteller must understand well. All too often,
storytellers start their stories in exposition, assuming that the audience needs to know
everything (or even something) before anything can happen.
Not true. Audiences want stories to start. They want to be thrust into the story. They are
willing to be taught things along the way, but get that story started and help the audience
catch as you move forward with action and dialogue.
Do the same for each scene of your story.
Don't start your restaurant scene with your arrival at the restaurant.
Don't start your airplane scene with the arrival at the airport.
Don't start your embarrassing high school locker room scene when you're fully clothed.
Start with a moment of meaning. Push into the story and each scene to a place that will grab
the audience's attention.
Start in medias res. Make the audience WANT to hear your story.
“Perform Like Steve Jobs” Workshop © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks Page: 3

-- 4 of 13 --

Lesson 3: Use "I" Stories for Impact
Jobs strategically tells an "I" story rather than a "we" story to enhance impact. This
personalizes the narrative, making it more compelling for the audience. No one wants to
hear what a group of people are thinking or feeling. Not only does universal “group think”
and “group feel” not exist, but the use of the word “we” lowers stakes and strips storytellers
of their vulnerability.
Stories are not told about collective groups. They are told about the protagonists for whom
the audience can affix their hopes and dreams.
TIP: STAY IN THE FIRST PERSON AT ALL TIMES
I was working with a storyteller whose grandmother passes away in the middle of the story.
In order to inform his audience about her death, he shifts perspective and presents the
scene in which his brother finds their grandmother lying in bed, having peacefully passed
away in her sleep.
But this is a mistake. The story is being told through the eyes of the storyteller, so shifting to
a scene where the storyteller was not present is both jarring and breaks the point of view. It
emotionally detaches the audience from the storyteller. It severs the all-too-important
connection that the storyteller and the audience have established.
Instead, I told the storyteller to inform the audience about the death of his grandmother in the
same way he learned about it:
Through a phone call from his brother.
The details of his brother finding their grandmother can still be told to the audience, but they
can be told via the phone call, which maintains the storyteller's emotional attachment to the
audience. In fact, it's a more dramatic, emotionally impactful scene when described through
the lens of the phone call. The audience gets to experience the emotional impact of the
news in the same way the storyteller did. Rather than viewing a scene through the lens of
something the audience doesn't know, they get to witness the actual experience of the
storyteller.
This is what audiences want. They want to understand a storyteller's experience. They want
to know what the storyteller thought and how they felt. Staying in the first person and
allowing the story to unfold through the eyes of the storyteller is the first step in achieving
this goal.
“Perform Like Steve Jobs” Workshop © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks Page: 4

-- 5 of 13 --

Lesson 4: Mistakes and Recovery
Even in the most critical presentations, Jobs makes a mistake, jumping a sentence at the
very beginning of his presentation. However, his quick recovery showcases the importance
of preparation.
Performers are prepared for mistakes and can seamlessly continue.
His mistake is handled so masterfully that almost no one has ever noticed it, even though in
comes in the third sentence of his performance.
TIP: IMPERFECTION IN STORYTELLING
As I watched the Super Bowl halftime show on a Sunday in 2020, I found myself wondering
if Jennifer Lopez and Shakira were lip-syncing their performance.
It sure looked like it.
I turned to the internet for answers and discovered that most Super Bowl halftime performers
lip-sync at least some of their acts. So, too, does the person singing the national anthem.
The NFL actually recommends it.
There was also a great deal of backlash on the internet about this practice. Earlier in the
week, Lady Gaga even warned Shakira and Lopez that she "didn't want to see any lip
syncing," and fans traditionally despise the practice.
I attended a Britney Spears concert years ago, and that entire show was lip-synced. I hated
every minute of it.
I found myself wondering:
Would fans prefer a sonically flawless performance that is pre-recorded and lip-synced, or
would they prefer a performance that is far from perfect but performed live?
I think they would almost always prefer the latter because the latter allows for authenticity
and vulnerability. It's real. We like to watch live events because the performer is putting their
soul on the line. Whether it's a football player or comic or trapeze artist, that person is
performing live, and anything could happen.
Performing live requires expertise and skill and courage. It's the courage we admire most.
As human beings, we are drawn to vulnerability and the bravery that it requires. The courage
to put everything on the line in a public way.
“Perform Like Steve Jobs” Workshop © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks Page: 5

-- 6 of 13 --

I think the same thing about storytelling. When a storyteller takes the stage and shares a bit
of her life, the audience is drawn to the vulnerability on display.
This is one of many reasons why I advise storytellers not to memorize their stories word-
for-word. Audiences do not want word callers reciting perfectly memorized lines to an
audience. They do not want monologists. They want to feel like the storyteller is speaking
from the heart. This is why we tell stories without notes, and it's why there is an enormous
difference between a writer reading a story to the audience and a storyteller telling a story to
the audience.
Lesson 5: The Historical Journey
Jobs takes the audience on a journey through Apple's history, showcasing the Macintosh
and iPod. This is what I call “universe building.” He is establishing Planet Apple and ensuring
that we all understand its terrain.
This not only builds a connection with his audience and level-sets everyone watching, but it
also establishes and reinforces Apple's expertise and success over the years.
Lesson 6: Utilize Repetition for Rhythm and
Emphasis
Repetition is a powerful tool, adding rhythm and emphasis to Jobs' storytelling. By repeating
phrases like "changed everything," he creates a musical quality that captivates the audience.
Poetry and song use repetition for these reasons.
Great storytellers so the same.
TIP: ALWAYS KEEP 'EM GUESSING
Basketball coach Doug Oliver famously said, "Always keep em' guessing."
Good advice in hoops. Better advice in storytelling.
I've recently been listening to stories for which the endings were abundantly clear. Halfway
through the story, I already knew how these stories would end.
No surprise whatsoever. This is not good.
If you're telling the story of your attempt to run a marathon, it should not be clear halfway
through your story that you will succeed.
“Perform Like Steve Jobs” Workshop © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks Page: 6

-- 7 of 13 --

If you're telling the story about asking a girl to the prom, it should not be obvious well before
the finish that the girl says yes.
If you're telling a story about applying to Harvard, your audience should not know that your
blood runs crimson red long before you speak your last word.
To keep an audience wondering, the ending cannot be all but certain halfway through your
story. You must find a way to "Always keep 'em guessing."
False stakes, misdirection, and the creation of a possibility tree are all excellent strategies to
keep the ending of your story obscured and uncertain.I can teach you how to do all of these
and many more.
Lesson 7: Importance of Content Ordering
Here lies Jobs’ only error in the opening of his keynote. Ordering content is crucial for
eliciting the desired audience response. Jobs' misordering of the “three new devices”
highlights the significance of understanding audience reactions.
It’s not a damaging mistake to the performance, but it represents a missed opportunity.
TIP: THE IMPORTANCE OF SET PIECES IN STORYTELLING
In the film, set pieces are the large, complex, memorable scenes that everyone remembers
when thinking about the movie.
The T-Rex attack in Jurassic Park (among many others).
The storming of the beaches on D-Day in Saving Private Ryan. The wedding dress shopping
gone bad scene in Bridesmaids.
Set pieces are enormously important in film, and they are just as important in storytelling,
too.
In order to take advantage of these potential set pieces in your stories, you need to allow
these moments to expand and breathe. These are the moments when the storyteller must
slow down the action so that the audience to get a sense of the importance of the moment
and enjoy the scene for all it has to offer.
In a recent storytelling show, these were moments when the storyteller was slapped in the
face by a camp counselor and a moment in a closet where a little girl was staring at a single
lightbulb, dangling on a chain, waiting to see if it would turn off.
“Perform Like Steve Jobs” Workshop © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks Page: 7

-- 8 of 13 --

The whole story hinged on whether or not this lightbulb went dark.
In both of these instances, I advised the storytellers to take these pivotal, set-piece
moments, and make them special. Give them more time. Slow things down. Offer the
audience some inner dialogue. Add details to bring the scene into full clarity.
Build suspense. Maximize surprise.
The first step is to simply identify these potential set pieces in your story - unforgettable,
absolutely critical moments that people will never forget. The singular moments that
audiences will remember long after you are finished telling your story.
You want your audience to remember these moments in the same way we remember the
T-Rex attack in Jurassic Park, the Allied landing in Normandy in Saving Private Ryan, and
the image of Maya Rudolph, collapsed in the middle of the road in her wedding dress,
pooping her heart out.
Make your set-piece scenes just as memorable in your stories by setting them apart from the
rest.
Make them special.
Indicate to the audience that they are special.
Lesson 8: Masterful Use of Slides: Less Text,
More Impact
Jobs' slides are minimalistic, containing only essential information. With five words or less,
each slide complements his speech, ensuring the audience's focus remains on him rather
than the visuals.
This is the perfect example of slides supporting a story instead of slides telling the story.
TIP: HERE'S A WAY TO GRAB AN AUDIENCE'S ATTENTION
In a story I'm developing, I plan to begin with a line like, "The scissors hidden in my lap are
sharp. Sharper than I expected. Now I wait for the teacher to turn his back so I can use
them."
The most suspenseful object in the scene - and perhaps the story - is the pair of scissors, so
I lead with them to create wonder, suspense, and even worry.
“Perform Like Steve Jobs” Workshop © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks Page: 8

-- 9 of 13 --

Why are you hiding scissors in my lap?
What do I plan on doing once the teacher turns his back? Why are they so sharp?
The scissors play a very minor role in the story, but I lead with them to grab the audience's
attention. I force my audience to wonder what will happen next. String enough "I wonder
what will happen next" moments together, and you've got yourself a story.
Lesson 9: Leverage Surprise and Suspense
for Maximum Impact
Jobs strategically introduces three revolutionary products, building suspense and surprise
throughout his performance.
Suspense is achieved through the simultaneous and strategic presenting of certain facts
coupled with the withholding of others.
Surprise is achieved through misdirection. Jobs plays on audience assumptions, misleads
the audience through the careful establishment of a false future, and then allows the
audience to realize the surprise before speaking the words himself, thus bringing delight to
his audience and making them feel like a part of the performance.
TIP: SURPRISE IS ESSENTIAL FOR GOOD STORYTELLING
I talk a lot about surprise in storytelling because I believe that it's through surprise that
almost every emotional response from our audiences is generated.
Laughter. Tears. Shock. Outrage. Disgust. Despair.
They all come from our ability to surprise our audience.
To that end, we must identify moments of potential surprise in our stories and allow them to
be as surprising as they can possibly be.
I recently helped two storytellers enhance surprising moments in their stories by removing
summation from their stories and allowing the surprise to be as visually startling as possible
for the audience.
In one story, a woman is wondering if her father will show up for her wedding. I advised the
storyteller to create as much certainty as possible in the minds of the audience that he
wasn't going to show up at the wedding, so that when he did arrive, it was truly startling to
the audience.
“Perform Like Steve Jobs” Workshop © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks Page: 9

-- 10 of 13 --

But the moment of revelation was the key, and in her first draft, she said something like,
"Finally, I look to the hill, and there on top, standing beside my brother, was my father."
See how summative that sentence is? The word "Finally" gives away the surprise, and it's
not the word you want to use when revealing this news to your audience. "Finally" is a word
that summarizes the moment. It's past tense. It's a big old signal that Dad is about to arrive
in a few seconds. The surprise has now been revealed, not with the image of her father but
with an adverb.
Instead, just make Dad arrive. Let us see Dad in the same way the storyteller did that day.
Say something like:
"I accept the fact that my father isn't coming, but that's okay. I have the man I love with all my
heart with my on this day. My soon-to-be husband. He will always be enough. I look to the
hill to get my brother's attention, so we can get this show started, and there he is. Standing
on the crest of the hill. My father. My father. Standing beside my brother, smiling. Dressed
and ready for my wedding."
See how the words "My father" become the reveal now? See how the surprise becomes an
image in your mind? A shocking, startling image if you didn't already know what was coming.
Spoken by a good storyteller, with the right pause and shift in tonality at just the right
moment, tears will spill from audience's eyes when these words are spoken.
Hell, I got choked up just writing them.
In another story, a storyteller is trying to avoid losing control on a motorcycle.
He says something like, "I nearly lost control twice as the bike slid across the road."
Instead of telling us that there were two moments of potential excitement and surprise, show
us those moments. Let us see the bike going over to one side. Let us feel what that is like.
Let us know what you are thinking. Make us believe that the crash is going to happen.
Then surprise us by regaining control of the bike. Twice.
Allow your audience to be in the moment with images, internal dialogue, and the certainty of
disaster just before the reveal. Rather than telling us that you almost crashed your bike
twice, let us see you almost crash your bike twice. Allow us to experience those moments
first- hand.
This particular story is actually filled with well-executed surprises. I was just happy to help
the storyteller add a couple more.
We must avoid summation in storytelling. Audiences want to experience stories in real time.
Not through the retrospective words of a storyteller in the future who knows what is coming.
This is most important when revealing a surprise. Always, always, always make that
revealing moment an image. Plant a picture in the minds of your audience when revealing
something as important and precious as a surprise.
Don't spoil surprises with summation.
“Perform Like Steve Jobs” Workshop © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks Page: 10

-- 11 of 13 --

Lesson 10: Make ‘Em Laugh
Jobs deploys humor - an actual sight gag - in his performance, and it represents one of the
most effective uses of a slide.
Everyone in business wants to be funny, but so few people in business are willing to try to be
funny. Instead, they want “to have been funny.”
Jobs makes a joke. He takes a chance on it falling flat, as jokes sometimes do. But
audiences love to laugh, and people who make them laugh as perceived as likable,
trustworthy, and intelligent.
The best things about humor:
1. So few people even attempt to be amusing in business.
2. Humor is a skill that can be learned.
3. Being funny can set you apart from the competition and your fellow speakers.
Lesson 11: Everyday Language
Jobs does not attempt to elevate his vocabulary. He uses everyday speech, which makes
him sound relatable, understandable, and believable.
Performers should always use their own language and avoid words that make them sound
like spokespeople, corporate monoliths, or neutral arbiters.
Be yourself.
TIP: CHOOSE WORDS THAT SAY A LOT OF THINGS
The shorter the story, almost always the better the story, so choosing words that mean a lot
of things can be a powerful and efficient way to tell a story.
Recently one of my corporate clients was telling a story about falling in love with Vietnam
culture during a college trip.
He said, "When the trip was over, I rushed back to school to change my life."
I loved the use of the word "rushed." It expressed urgency, excitement, and an absolute
imperative. A simple verb said so much.
When you find the right word, you need not say a bunch of other words.
“Perform Like Steve Jobs” Workshop © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks Page: 11

-- 12 of 13 --

Conclusion: Steve Jobs as a Performer and
Master Storyteller
Steve Jobs' Macworld 2007 iPhone launch is a testament to his skills as a performer and
master storyteller. By decoding his presentation, we uncover valuable lessons in confidence,
stake-setting, personalization, repetition, slide design, and the art of surprise. Implementing
these lessons can significantly enhance one's ability to captivate and persuade audiences in
business presentations.
“Perform Like Steve Jobs” Workshop © Storyworthy by Matthew Dicks Page: 12
